assessment.summary: A recurring space-weather hazard with documented power-grid and GPS disruptions already costing hundreds of millions to tens of billions of dollars; an extreme, Carrington-scale storm could cause continental-scale infrastructure damage.
assessment.timeframe: Moderate geomagnetic storms occur multiple times per solar cycle (roughly every 11 years); an extreme, Carrington-scale event is rare but recurs on a multi-decade to century timescale.

claims.text: The Carrington Event of 1859 sent charged particles crashing through Earth's magnetic field, causing telegraph systems to spark and fail; NOAA notes that a storm of that magnitude hitting today could cripple communication networks, disrupt the power grid, and cost the global economy trillions of dollars.

A 2020 study found that NOAA space weather forecasts help the electric power industry avoid losses ranging from $111 million for minor geomagnetic disturbances to as much as $27 billion for severe geomagnetic storms; the May 2024 G5 (Extreme) geomagnetic storm caused a GPS outage during a crucial planting period that cost American farmers over $500 million in potential profit.

description: A powerful coronal mass ejection from the Sun can trigger a severe geomagnetic storm that induces damaging currents in power grids, disrupts satellites and GPS, and degrades radio and aviation communications across large regions simultaneously. Moderate storms occur regularly; the largest storms on record, such as the 1859 Carrington Event, are rare but would affect infrastructure far more dependent on electronics and satellites today than in the past.
name: Severe Geomagnetic Storm (Solar Storm)
